name: Broken Object Property Level Authorization (BOPLA)
what_it_means: The API accepts or returns object properties that the requesting user should not be able to read or modify — combining Excessive Data Exposure (returning too many fields) and Mass Assignment (accepting fields that should be server-controlled). Attackers exploit this to escalate privileges, access sensitive data, or tamper with protected attributes.
animated_example: Filling a form where hidden admin checkboxes are still clickable if you know their names — the UI hides them, but the server accepts anything you submit.
quick_checks: Strict request schema validation enforced — extra or sensitive properties rejected with 400
Response allowlists/DTOs in place — only necessary fields returned per role
Field-level authorization enforced for create and update operations
Framework mass-assignment disabled by default; explicit writeable property maps defined per endpoint
Sensitive flags (isAdmin, roles, verified, creditLimit) are server-controlled only and excluded from all user input binding
API response schema diffing runs in CI/CD to catch accidental field exposure before deployment

payload_types.type: Mass Assignment via Extra Request Fields
payload_types.description: Attacker injects additional properties into a POST/PUT/PATCH request body that map directly to privileged model fields, exploiting frameworks that automatically bind all incoming JSON properties to database model objects.
examples: POST /api/users { "name": "Ana", "isAdmin": true } — framework binds isAdmin directly to the user model, granting admin privileges
PUT /api/profile { "email": "new@mail.com", "role": "admin", "creditLimit": 999999 } — all three extra fields accepted and persisted
POST /api/register { "username": "bob", "password": "pass", "verified": true } — bypasses email verification step
PATCH /api/account { "subscriptionTier": "enterprise", "trialExpiry": "2099-01-01" } — modifies billing fields without authorization
POST /api/orders { "productId": 5, "quantity": 1, "price": 0.01 } — overrides server-calculated price with attacker-supplied value
mitigation: Disable framework-level mass assignment by default — explicitly whitelist every bindable field per endpoint using allowlist schemas (Rails strong_parameters, Spring @JsonIgnoreProperties, Django read_only_fields).
Use dedicated Data Transfer Objects (DTOs) for every request that contain only the fields the endpoint is permitted to accept — never bind directly to ORM/database model objects.
Mark sensitive fields (isAdmin, roles, balance, verified, subscriptionTier) as server-controlled: calculate or set them in business logic only, never from request input.
Return 400 Bad Request for any request containing properties not on the input allowlist — do not silently ignore extra fields as this masks attack attempts.
Automate schema validation in integration tests: submit requests with all known sensitive fields set to attacker-controlled values and assert they are rejected or ignored.

payload_types.type: Excessive Data Exposure in API Responses
payload_types.description: API returns more object properties than the requesting user is entitled to see — including internal flags, sensitive PII, hashed credentials, or other users' data — relying on the client to filter what is displayed rather than the server to restrict what is returned.
examples: GET /api/users/42 returns { id, name, email, passwordHash, internalNotes, isAdmin, stripeCustomerId } — all fields returned regardless of caller's role
GET /api/orders returns full order objects including cardLast4, billingAddress, and internalFraudScore for all users
Search API returns full user profiles including phone numbers and date of birth when only name and email should be exposed
API response includes __v, _id, createdBy, updatedBy, internalStatus fields from the ORM layer that were never intended for external consumption
GET /api/profile returns other users' data fields when a related-object query is over-fetched without ownership filtering
mitigation: Define explicit response DTOs (allowlisted field sets) for every endpoint per caller role — serialize only fields on the allowlist before returning the response.
Never return ORM model objects directly to clients — always pass through a serializer or projection layer that strips internal and sensitive fields.
Implement role-aware field filtering: admin callers may receive additional fields, but standard users receive the minimal field set required for their use case.
Automate response schema validation in CI/CD: compare actual API response fields against the documented schema and fail the build if undocumented fields are returned.
Apply data classification to all model fields and use the classification to drive the response serializer — fields marked 'internal' or 'sensitive' are never included in external API responses.

payload_types.type: Privilege Escalation via Sensitive Property Injection
payload_types.description: Attacker submits a request body containing privilege-elevating properties (isAdmin, role, permissions) that the server accepts and persists, granting unauthorized elevated access.
examples: PATCH /api/users/me { "role": "admin" } accepted — user escalates their own role to admin
POST /api/invitations { "email": "x@y.com", "permissions": ["read","write","admin"] } — attacker grants admin permission to an invited user
PUT /api/teams/5/members/99 { "role": "owner" } — non-owner member escalates another member to team owner
POST /api/subscriptions { "plan": "free", "features": ["export","api_access","white_label"] } — injects premium features into a free plan subscription
POST /api/tasks { "title": "task", "assignedToUserId": 1 } — regular user assigns a task to the admin account, gaining visibility into admin workflow
mitigation: Treat all privilege-related fields (role, isAdmin, permissions, tier, features) as server-controlled — calculate them from authenticated session context and business rules, never from request input.
Apply field-level authorization checks: validate that the requesting user has the privilege required to set each submitted field value, not just to call the endpoint.
Audit all PATCH and PUT endpoints for fields that map to authorization-relevant model attributes and confirm they are excluded from the input binding.
Log all changes to privilege-related fields with before/after values, actor identity, and timestamp — alert on self-privilege-escalation attempts.
Include privilege injection test cases in your automated security test suite: attempt to set every known privilege field on every writable endpoint as a low-privilege user and assert rejection.

payload_types.description: Attacker exploits partial update endpoints (PATCH) to modify fields that were intended as read-only or server-controlled, or uses a full PUT to overwrite fields not explicitly included in the payload with default/null values.
examples: PATCH /api/invoices/99 { "status": "paid" } — regular user marks an invoice as paid without completing the payment flow
PATCH /api/users/42 { "emailVerified": true } — bypasses email verification by directly setting the flag
PUT /api/products/5 with a partial body — fields not included in the PUT body are reset to null/defaults, corrupting unrelated product data
PATCH /api/contracts/10 { "signedAt": "2020-01-01", "signedBy": "legit_user" } — backdates a contract signature
PATCH /api/audits/7 { "findings": [] } — attacker clears an audit log by submitting an empty findings array
mitigation: Define separate input schemas for PATCH operations that explicitly list only the fields a caller is permitted to modify — never allow PATCH to touch server-controlled or sensitive fields.
For PUT operations, reconstruct the full object server-side from the existing record merged with only the allowlisted fields from the request — never do a full model replacement from user input.
Apply field-level authorization to every field in a PATCH body: check that the caller has permission to modify that specific field, not just permission to call the endpoint.
Reject PATCH requests that include read-only fields (status, createdAt, verifiedAt, signedAt) with a 400 error identifying the offending field.
Log all partial update operations with a diff of changed fields and actor identity — alert on changes to sensitive state fields (status, verified, signed) by non-privileged users.

payload_types.description: Attacker crafts GraphQL queries requesting fields beyond their authorization level, exploiting missing field-level resolvers or authorization checks that only gate the top-level query but not individual nested fields.
examples: { user(id: 42) { id, email, passwordHash, internalNotes, stripeToken } } — sensitive fields returned because only the user resolver is gated, not individual fields
{ me { id, orders { id, cardLast4, billingAddress { fullAddress } } } } — payment details returned via nested resolver with no field-level authorization
{ users { id, role, isAdmin, twoFactorSecret } } — admin-only fields returned to standard user because the users list query has no field-level restriction
{ product(id: 5) { id, name, costPrice, supplierName, internalMargin } } — internal pricing data exposed via unrestricted field resolution
Introspection query revealing all available fields and types, including sensitive internal fields not intended for external callers
mitigation: Implement field-level authorization resolvers for every sensitive field — do not rely solely on top-level query authorization to protect nested or related object fields.
Use a GraphQL authorization middleware (graphql-shield, Graphene permissions) that enforces per-field access rules derived from the authenticated user's role.
Disable or restrict GraphQL introspection in production — if introspection is required, gate it behind admin authentication.
Apply query depth and complexity limits to prevent over-fetching of nested objects that individually pass field authorization but collectively expose sensitive data.
Audit your GraphQL schema for fields with sensitive data classifications and ensure each has an explicit authorization resolver before deploying schema changes.

how_to_test: Step 1: Submit POST/PUT/PATCH requests with extra fields (isAdmin, role, verified, creditLimit) and assert the server rejects or ignores them — verify the values are not persisted in a subsequent GET
Step 2: Retrieve objects as a low-privilege user and inspect every field in the response — compare against the documented schema and flag any undocumented or sensitive fields
Step 3: Attempt privilege escalation by submitting role/permissions fields in update requests as a standard user — assert 400 or that the field is silently stripped and not persisted
Step 4: Test PATCH endpoints with read-only fields (status, verifiedAt, signedAt) — assert 400 is returned with the offending field identified
Step 5: Send a PUT request with a partial body omitting required fields — assert the server does not null out the omitted fields on the stored record
Step 6: Issue GraphQL queries requesting sensitive fields (passwordHash, internalNotes, stripeToken) as a standard user — assert fields are null or absent, not returned
Step 7: Run automated response schema diffing between environments to detect any fields newly appearing in API responses that are not on the approved schema
expected_outcomes: Requests containing extra or privileged fields return 400 Bad Request — sensitive fields are never accepted from user input
API responses contain only the fields on the approved schema for the caller's role — no internal flags, hashed credentials, or ORM metadata
Privilege-escalation attempts via property injection are rejected and logged
PATCH operations on read-only or server-controlled fields are rejected with a descriptive 400 error
GraphQL queries for sensitive fields return null or are rejected based on field-level authorization resolvers
minimal_example: POST /users { name: 'Ana', role: 'admin' } → 400 field not permitted; GET /users/42 response contains only { id, name, email } — never passwordHash, isAdmin, or internal flags
mitigation: Define explicit allow-listed DTOs for every request and response — reject or return 400 for any property not on the schema allowlist; never bind request bodies directly to ORM model objects
Enforce field-level authorization: sensitive fields (isAdmin, roles, balance, creditLimit, verified) must be server-controlled only and excluded from all user-writable input binding
Disable framework mass-assignment by default (Rails strong_parameters, Spring @JsonIgnoreProperties, Django read_only_fields) and explicitly whitelist every bindable property per endpoint
Automate API response schema diffing in CI/CD pipelines to detect accidental exposure of new fields; implement field-level GraphQL authorization resolvers for all sensitive schema fields
